food_subgroup: Poultry offal and extremities — braised/marinated duck feet

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for duck feet or Sichuan-style braised duck feet. FOODON_03315763 (duck food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers duck meat and offal as a category. No specific code exists for braised spicy duck feet; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for duck feet as a food item. SNOMED CT coverage for specific prepared poultry extremity dishes is absent.

preparation_method: spiced with Sichuan peppercorn and chilli

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Sichuan Spicy Duck Feet with Coriander consists of approximately 4 to 5 duck feet, weighing around 150 g, served as a starter or side dish in a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, feet, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; adapted with reference to USDA NDB No. 05148 (Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted) and peer-reviewed compositional data for braised poultry collagen-rich cuts
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Sichuan-style braised duck feet with coriander. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy duck cooked entries (NDB 05148, duck meat and skin, roasted) and compositional literature on duck feet, which are high in collagen/gelatin, moderate in fat, and low in carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated to reflect soy sauce and seasoning typical of this preparation. Carbohydrate is low reflecting minimal starch addition. Values represent a best-estimate composite for the prepared dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of high-protein, low-carbohydrate braised poultry dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for braised meat dishes and collagen-rich poultry preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sichuan braised duck feet. GI is estimated at 25 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate braised poultry and meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains approximately 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from soy sauce and minor sugar additions; the protein and collagen matrix substantially blunts glycemic response. GL per 150 g serving = (25 × 5.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~620 mg per 100 g; ~930 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from soy sauce-based braising; a single 150 g serving may contribute approximately 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~25) with minimal glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sichuan Spicy Duck Feet with Coriander has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load, making it a relatively safe protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though sodium content warrants monitoring for those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~620 mg per 100 g); cholesterol ~78 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The moderate saturated fat and high sodium content of this dish are relevant concerns for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be occasional and portion-controlled, and lower-sodium preparation methods should be requested where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sichuan Spicy Duck Feet with Coriander (川香麻辣鸭掌, Chuān Xiāng Málà Yā Zhǎng) is a classic Sichuan-style Chinese dish featuring duck feet that are braised and marinated in a bold, numbing-spicy sauce characteristic of Sichuan cuisine. The dish is prepared by slow-braising cleaned duck feet in a fragrant broth of soy sauce, Sichuan peppercorn (huājiāo), dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, star anise, five-spice powder, rice wine, and a small amount of sugar, then finishing with sesame oil and a generous garnish of fresh coriander (cilantro). Duck feet are prized in Chinese culinary tradition for their gelatinous, collagen-rich texture — the skin and tendons become tender and yielding after braising, while the bones provide structural contrast. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, spicy, and numbing (málà), with the coriander providing a fresh herbal counterpoint. Nutritionally, duck feet are high in collagen-derived protein and moderate in fat, with very low carbohydrate content. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 195 kcal, 16.5 g protein, 13.2 g fat, and only 3.5 g carbohydrate, making it a low-glycemic food (GI ~25). Sodium content is elevated (~620 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce-based braising, which is a key consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is commonly served as a starter, cold appetiser, or side dish in Chinese restaurants in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding soy or sesame allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sichuan-style braised duck feet exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy duck cooked entries and compositional literature on collagen-rich poultry extremities. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; no exact approved-source entry for Sichuan braised duck feet exists. USDA SR Legacy duck (cooked, meat and skin) was used as the primary compositional anchor, adjusted for the collagen-rich, skin-dominant nature of duck feet and the sodium contribution of soy sauce braising. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy duck entries used as the source basis. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting minor naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) present in poultry fat; this is not an industrial trans fat. 4. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 150 g serving calculated as (25 × 5.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.3. 5. Halal status is marked false as duck preparation and soy sauce sourcing cannot be confirmed as halal-certified for this specific restaurant offering. 6. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy duck cooked data and are approximate. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database.
